quizzes.title: W3 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Genesis 1:1–2, merism, land, and the opening interpretive framework of creation

questions.question: How does the lecture ultimately classify Genesis 1:1?

options.text: As a summary of the whole chapter

questions.explanation: The lecture argues Genesis 1:1 is best read as an indicative statement reporting God’s creation of the heavens and the earth.

questions.question: What structural reason is given for rejecting Genesis 1:1 as a normal title?

options.text: It is too short to be a title
Genesis usually uses the Toledot formula for headings

questions.explanation: The lecture says Genesis commonly marks sections with the Toledot formula, so Genesis 1:1 does not function like the book’s standard headings.

questions.question: In the lecture, 'heavens and earth' is treated as what kind of expression?
questions.explanation: The phrase is explained as a merism, using two extremes to communicate the whole.

questions.question: Which rendering does the lecture prefer for 'eretz' as Genesis 1 narrows its focus?
questions.explanation: The lecture argues 'land' better preserves the Pentateuch’s thematic connection to garden and promised-land themes.

questions.question: How does the lecture interpret 'tohu vavohu' in Genesis 1:2?
questions.explanation: The phrase is explained as waste and wilderness rather than a metaphysical void.

questions.question: What role does the Spirit of God in Genesis 1:2 play in the lecture’s interpretation?

options.text: Only a poetic image with no later significance
A sign that creation is complete already
Part of God’s preparing a dwelling place fit for life
Proof that Genesis 1 is only allegory

questions.explanation: The Spirit is presented as actively involved in preparing and ordering creation as a place for God’s purposes and human habitation.

quizzes.title: W3 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Genesis 1 day structure, Hebrew wording, and the ordering of creation from days 1–6

questions.question: What is notable about the wording for the first day in Genesis 1:5 according to the lecture?

options.text: It uses the Hebrew ordinal for first
It uses a cardinal expression, 'day one'
It uses a plural form for days

questions.explanation: The lecture highlights that Genesis 1:5 says 'day one' with a cardinal number rather than an ordinal.

questions.question: How does the lecture describe the phrase 'let there be' in Genesis 1:3?

options.text: As a direct use of bara, create
As a Hebrew verb of being or presence

questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes that the wording points to being or appearance rather than explicitly using the verb 'create.'

questions.question: What tension does the lecture call attention to in the sequence of creation days?

options.text: Light appearing before the sun, moon, and stars

questions.explanation: A major interpretive issue raised is that light appears on day 1 while the luminaries are associated with day 4.

questions.question: Why does the lecture suggest day 2 may lack the statement 'God saw that it was good'?

options.text: Because day 2 records no creative act
Because of a literary pattern aiming at seven total 'good' statements

questions.explanation: The proposed explanation is literary design, with double 'good' statements on days 3 and 6 helping create a sevenfold pattern.

questions.question: How does the lecture describe the relationship between days 1–3 and days 4–6?

options.text: They are unrelated units from different authors
Days 1–3 form and separate; days 4–6 fill and govern
Days 1–3 are historical, days 4–6 are symbolic
Days 1–3 describe heaven, days 4–6 describe only earth

questions.explanation: The lecture presents the week as symmetrical: the first triad forms or separates, and the second triad fills or governs.

questions.question: How is dominion in Genesis 1 explained in the day six lecture?

options.text: As political monarchy over other humans
As a command limited to Eden only

questions.explanation: The lecture frames rule and subdue language as stewardship rather than abuse or exploitation.

questions.question: What literary feature especially spotlights humanity in Genesis 1:27?

options.text: A poetic section with repeated 'create' language

questions.explanation: Genesis 1:27 is treated as poetry, and the triple repetition of 'create' underscores humanity’s significance.

quizzes.title: W3 Quiz 3
quizzes.description: The seventh day, Sabbath rest, and the big idea of Genesis 1 within the Pentateuch

questions.question: What span does the lecture treat as the first major unit of the creation account?
questions.explanation: The lecture treats Genesis 1:1 through Genesis 2:3 as a coherent seven-day unit.

questions.question: How is the seventh day said to differ structurally from days 1–6?

options.text: It repeats the normal evening-morning formula twice
It introduces a second creation of humanity
It breaks the repeated pattern found in the earlier days

questions.explanation: Day seven is marked as unique because it lacks several of the standard repeated refrains used in days 1–6.

questions.question: Which theme is emphasized by the triple repetition noted in Genesis 2:1–3?
questions.explanation: The lecture highlights repeated completion language to stress that God’s work had been completed.

questions.question: What two actions especially mark the seventh day as holy?

options.text: God names it and fills it
God hides it and tests humanity in it
God blesses it and sanctifies it
God ends it and replaces it

questions.explanation: The day is set apart through divine blessing and sanctification.

questions.question: How does the lecture connect the Sabbath theme beyond Genesis 2?

options.text: It says the theme ends there
It points forward to Leviticus and Israel’s ongoing search for rest
It limits Sabbath only to Adam
It applies only to kingship language

questions.explanation: The lecture traces Sabbath and rest forward into the Pentateuch, especially Leviticus, and into Israel’s unresolved pursuit of rest.

questions.question: What interpretive question does the lecture raise at the end of the session?

options.text: Why Genesis uses Hebrew instead of Aramaic
Why Genesis 1 should be read next to Genesis 2
Whether the flood came before Eden

questions.explanation: The lecture ends by asking how we know the author intended Genesis 1 and Genesis 2 to be read together.
